ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef bowl / gyudon entry; the okra and dashi sauce variant with combo sides is not individually coded. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 combo set (gyudon bowl + half boiled egg + miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard combo serving consists of approximately 550 g total, comprising a gyudon rice bowl with okra and dashi sauce (approximately 400 g), one half boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef bowl) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite; supplemented with USDA okra and dashi stock component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for okra gyudon with dashi sauce combo. Values are estimated by component-weighted assembly: steamed white rice (USDA SR Legacy #20444), simmered beef slices (USDA SR Legacy #13365 cooked ground beef as proxy for thinly sliced simmered beef), okra boiled (USDA SR Legacy #11278), half boiled egg (USDA SR Legacy #01129), miso soup (USDA SR Legacy #16120 miso as base), and dashi stock (USDA SR Legacy #14429 fish stock as proxy). Proportions estimated from standard Sukiya-style gyudon combo composition: rice ~180 g, beef ~80 g, okra ~30 g, egg ~50 g, miso soup ~100 g, dashi sauce ~30 g, sauce/seasoning ~30 g. Per-100 g values derived from weighted blend of components divided by total serving weight of 500 g edible portion (excluding miso soup liquid for density calculation). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice and mixed beef-rice dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for okra gyudon with dashi sauce. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant glycemic driver being steamed Japanese white rice (GI ~65–72, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021 Am J Clin Nutr). The beef, okra, egg, and dashi components contribute negligible glycemic load. A blended GI of 65 is used as a conservative estimate anchored to the white rice GI lower bound, consistent with mixed rice-protein-fat meal attenuation effects documented in peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 33.4) / 100 ≈ 21.7, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving ≈ 33.4 g (based on 18.5 g per 100 g × 1.81 density factor for edible portion).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi stock, soy-based seasoning, and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this combo meal to occasional consumption, as the combined sodium from dashi sauce, soy-seasoned beef, and miso soup can contribute approximately 700–900 mg sodium per serving, representing 30–40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice base; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the white rice base contributes a medium GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 21.7 per serving; portion control and pairing with the egg and okra (which provide fibre and protein to blunt postprandial glucose rise) are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and egg yolk; elevated sodium from dashi and miso
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note that this meal contains moderate saturated fat (approximately 8.8 g per serving) from beef and egg, and elevated sodium from dashi and miso soup; choosing a smaller portion or substituting the miso soup with a low-sodium drink option can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Okra Gyudon with Dashi Sauce Combo is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish featuring thinly sliced, simmered beef and sliced okra served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, finished with a light, tangy dashi-based sauce. The combo set is typically served with a half boiled egg and a bowl of miso soup or a beverage. The dish originates from the gyudon tradition popularised by Japanese fast-food chains such as Sukiya and Yoshinoya, where thinly sliced beef is simmered in a sweet-savoury broth of soy sauce, mirin, and dashi stock. The addition of okra introduces a mild, slightly mucilaginous texture and a subtle vegetal flavour that complements the umami-rich beef and dashi sauce. The dashi sauce, made from kombu and bonito stock, adds a light, savoury, and slightly tangy finishing note that distinguishes this variant from standard gyudon. Nutritionally, this combo meal provides a balanced macronutrient profile with approximately 142 kcal per 100 g, moderate protein from beef and egg, and carbohydrates primarily from the white rice base. The sodium content is elevated due to the dashi sauce, soy seasoning, and miso soup, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven by the white rice component, with the protein, fat, and fibre from beef, egg, and okra providing some attenuation of postprandial glucose response. This dish is suitable for general consumption but requires portion awareness for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, soy, egg, or gluten allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for okra gyudon with dashi sauce combo. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted assembly using USDA SR Legacy entries for each constituent ingredient. GI is estimated by analogy to white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA SR Legacy data; no direct database entry exists for this specific dish. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported across the component sources used for estimation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g were estimated from component USDA SR Legacy entries and are reported as positive numeric estimates rather than -1 because sufficient component data was available to produce defensible estimates. No core macro is zero; all four core macros are positive and supported. GI is estimated at 65 (medium) by analogy to white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Sodium is elevated due to dashi, soy seasoning, and miso soup. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON (Japanese Curry, Grilled Chicken Skewers, Fried Chicken) appear to be platform-level category tags unrelated to this specific dish and have not been carried forward as food descriptors. Restaurant data from field survey data (Sukiya - Funan, Funan - Mix & Match) is preserved in provenance context. Price SGD 9.00 noted from source.
